title: First Evaluation Pass: A Warning Sign
concept: A model can post a high accuracy score and still be nearly useless when the classes are imbalanced — like StreamBox's 95% stay vs. 5% churn split — which is why precision, recall, and F1 matter far more than accuracy alone.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why did StreamBox choose Random Forest over Logistic Regression for churn prediction?

options.text: Better accuracy and more robustness to messy, non-linear features
It doesn't require any training data

exercises.explain: Right! Random Forest's accuracy and robustness to messy features made it the better fit here.

cards.title: 95% accuracy... wait
cards.body: The Random Forest model comes back with 95% accuracy and the team almost celebrates — until someone asks: what happens if the model just predicted "no churn" for every single customer?

cards.title: Precision, recall, and the trap of accuracy
cards.body: Precision asks: of the customers flagged as churn risks, how many actually churned? Recall asks: of the customers who actually churned, how many did the model catch? Accuracy alone can hide a model that's barely doing either.

cards.title: A smoke detector that never goes off
cards.body: A smoke detector that never sounds an alarm is "correct" on 99% of ordinary days — no fire, no alarm. But the one day there IS a fire, it's useless. High accuracy on days with no churn hides a model that may be missing the churn cases entirely.

cards.title: StreamBox's real numbers
cards.body: Out of 500,000 customers, 475,000 stayed (95%) and 25,000 churned (5%). A model that predicts "stay" for everyone scores 95% accuracy but catches zero actual churners. StreamBox's real Random Forest scores: precision 61%, recall 18%, F1-score 0.28 — it's missing over 80% of the customers who actually churn.

cards.title: "High accuracy means a good model" — not here
cards.body: You might assume 95% accuracy is a strong result. In reality, when one class (staying) outnumbers the other (churning) 19-to-1, a model can score high accuracy while barely detecting the class that actually matters — the churners.

cards.title: The Misleading Number vs. The Real Story
cards.body: Same model, two very different signals:

left.text:
Looks impressive
Inflated by the 95%
who never churn anyway

right.text:
The real warning sign
Misses 82% of
actual churners

exercises.prompt: Why is 95% accuracy misleading for StreamBox's churn model?

options.text: 95% of customers don't churn anyway, so a model could reach ~95% accuracy just by never predicting churn
Accuracy can never be calculated for classification models
The model has too many features
95% accuracy is always a perfect score

exercises.explain: Correct! With a 95/5 imbalance, always guessing 'no churn' alone nets ~95% accuracy without catching a single real churner.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A dataset where 95% of examples are one class and 5% are the other is called imbalanced.
exercises.explain: True! A lopsided class split like 95%/5% is the definition of imbalanced classes.

exercises.sentence: With classes split 95% stay vs. 5% churn, the dataset is described as ___, which makes accuracy a misleading metric.
exercises.explain: Imbalanced classes make raw accuracy look artificially high while hiding poor performance on the minority class.

exercises.prompt: Which metric actually reveals that StreamBox's model is missing most real churners?
exercises.explain: Correct! An 18% recall means the model catches only 18% of the customers who actually churn — accuracy hides this.

exercises.prompt: StreamBox's Random Forest scores 95% accuracy, but only 61% precision, 18% recall, and an F1-score of 0.28, on a dataset where 95% of customers stay and 5% churn. What does this combination of numbers actually reveal, and what should StreamBox do next?

options.text: The high accuracy is inflated by the class imbalance; the low recall shows the model misses most real churners, so StreamBox needs techniques that address imbalanced data before this model is useful.
The model is performing excellently across the board and is ready to launch immediately.
Precision, recall, and F1-score don't apply to imbalanced datasets, so accuracy is the only number that matters.
The model should be discarded entirely and replaced with a coin flip.

exercises.explain: Exactly! 95% accuracy is inflated by the lopsided class split, while the low recall exposes the real problem: the model misses most actual churners. That's the warning sign — next up, fixing imbalanced data and getting this model truly ready for the real world!
